finding: An individual attribute (less quantity) was judged more favorably than an alternative with greater quantity, but only under isolated evaluation; the effect reversed when options were presented side-by-side.
Affective reactions to a stimulus mediated the inverse relationship between risk and benefit judgments; time pressure strengthened this mediation.
Exposure to a partner's information during discussion increased conformity errors in later recall, but conformity arose primarily from perceived accuracy rather than compliance.
An individual attribute (belief type) was associated with more liberal decision criteria in a detection task, leading to higher false alarm rates despite no difference in underlying sensitivity.
An attribute of the target (singularity) moderated the magnitude of response to individual identification; emotional reaction mediated this effect.
An incidental contextual cue affected numerical estimates even when the cue was not recalled and across expertise levels.
A spatial metaphor-valence association found in one cultural group was reversed in another cultural group, demonstrating cultural moderation of a supposedly universal cognitive mapping.
Mere ownership of an attribute (own name letters) increased preference for those items across multiple linguistic contexts.
An attribute of the target (shared vs. non-shared group identity) moderated how observers categorized the target's identity based on the target's performance outcome.
Exposure to one stimulus type (delayed reinforcement) reduced preference for immediate rewards, whereas exposure to the alternative (immediate reinforcement) did not increase that preference.
An individual attribute (stated preference) was predictive of behavioral effort expended in a physical response task.
A multidimensional construct (intergenerational climate perceptions) was associated with outcomes including mentoring behavior and job satisfaction.
Performance relative to prior expectations affected satisfaction judgments; this effect was moderated by individual differences.
Individual attributes (personality traits) were associated with body weight measures both cross-sectionally and prospectively, with gender moderating some associations.
A dimensional construct (narcissism) could be decomposed into categorical subgroups that differed in their associations with adjustment outcomes.
An individual attribute (personality disposition) moderated how an affective prime influenced incidental memory for related stimuli.
An individual attribute (optimism) was associated with lower stress prospectively; perceived but not objectively reported availability of social support mediated this effect.
Competition from one source (informal firms) increased a strategic response (new product development), with this effect moderated by the strength of alternative competitive and institutional factors.
An individual attribute (gender) moderated how a framing manipulation (give vs. take frame) affected allocation behavior.
Communication increased capacity for collective coordination and cooperation in a two-level provision dilemma with asymmetric constraints.
